context: Current closing price of United Airlines (UAL): $91.71 USD as of 2026-04-22.
justification_text:
My point estimates reflect a modestly upward-drifting baseline from an assumed current price around 92–93, with the 1-day estimate of 92.5 essentially anchored to the last observed level given that intraday mean reversion tends to dominate over very short horizons. The 1-week estimate of 93.0 nudges slightly higher, incorporating a mild positive drift assumption consistent with whatever underlying trend was present at the time of the forecast. The 1-month estimate of 95.0 extrapolates that drift further while also acknowledging that over longer horizons, fundamental valuation and macro tailwinds or headwinds begin to matter more than short-term momentum. In all three cases, I was essentially treating the asset as having a slightly positive expected return with no strong catalyst in either direction dominating the near-term outlook.

The confidence interval widths grow appropriately with the forecast horizon, reflecting the square-root-of-time scaling that characterizes diffusive price processes. At 1 day, the 90% interval spans roughly 12 points, which is relatively tight and reflects the low realized volatility typical of a single trading session absent a major surprise. By 1 week the interval widens to 26 points, and by 1 month it reaches 54 points, capturing the compounding uncertainty from sequential daily moves as well as the increasing likelihood that new macroeconomic data, earnings announcements, or policy decisions will shift the fundamental picture meaningfully. I also allowed for some additional fat-tail padding beyond pure Gaussian diffusion, since asset prices routinely exhibit jump behavior that pure volatility estimates understate.

Several specific risks could push outcomes well outside these stated intervals. On the downside, an unexpected hawkish policy shock, a deterioration in credit conditions, or a geopolitical escalation could produce a sharp selloff that breaches the lower bound even within a single day. On the upside, a surprise positive catalyst such as a strong economic data release, a dovish central bank pivot, or a short-squeeze dynamic could push the price well above the upper end of any horizon's interval. Liquidity crises or correlated asset unwinds are particularly dangerous because they can produce non-linear gap moves that no volatility-based interval adequately captures. The 1-month interval is especially vulnerable to these tail events, as a full month provides ample time for a regime change that would invalidate the drift and volatility assumptions underlying the entire forecast structure.
